blocks.content: His flight was Tuesday. Hers was Thursday — same as last year, the same drive with her mom. Monday night they sat on her porch the way they always had, except now there was no word for what they were holding back because they weren't holding anything back.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: Last year this felt like going off a cliff.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: And now.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: Now it feels like a pause. We know how to do the distance. We've already done it.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: We did it without a name. We can do it with one.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: Thursdays and Sundays.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: And whenever else.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: And whenever else.
She shifted closer. He put his arm around her. The neighborhood was going dark around them.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: Go make the thing you need to make.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: I will.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: And then come home.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: And then come home.
He kissed her temple. She leaned into it. The porch light hummed the way it always had.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: I love you.

He'd said versions of it before — in the field, in December, in every phone call that went long past midnight. But not those words. Not exactly. This was the plain true thing, said out loud on a porch in August, with nothing left to almost.
She looked at him.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: I love you, Kit.
Plain. True. Ten years in the making and worth every single one of them.

Millhaven went quiet around them. They stayed exactly where they were for as long as they could — and when the night finally asked them to let go, they did, knowing exactly what was waiting on the other side of the distance.
